comments.text: Squirrels in pine forests on Saint Helena? Seriously? The island has no native land mammals and yet you include that at the very beginning. I didn't watch any further...

comments.text: Seems like somebody wrote a script for the robovoice,  then  they put it alongside the video without much attempt to make them match.,

comments.author: @traveldocumentaries4k
comments.text:
No you can't buy a house there.
No you can't live there.
You can't alight onto the island without permission.
They have Internet.
They sell traditional crafts and specialty knitwear.
Beware of the tourist officer called Randall Repetto.
He likes to befriend you and eventually after a few messages back and forth, will ask you for money. Complaining he has a poor wage, but this is a decent wage pro rata and the economy is subsidized to all the residents to be able to afford to travel should they need to, especially if medically evacuated. 
The UK pays for the subsidy of TDC. 
They have a good life there with many of the young people choosing to stay instead of leaving for a life abroad. 
So Randall could leave anytime he wants, he goes abroad quite often for holidays so can afford that. 
He's in his 20s and chooses to stay on TDC.
Around Xmas time he will ask for money and goods. 
You have been warned.

comments.author: @NovaTerrain-4K
comments.text: The documentary dives deep into the lives of the "Saints," their "Island Time" mindset, sustainable pole-and-line tuna fishing, and their world-famous, ultra-rare Green Tipped Bourbon coffee.

comments.author: @EpicEarthYTB
comments.text: Saint Helena is a hidden gem with such a rich history and breathtaking landscapes. It is truly a unique corner of the world that I’d love to visit one day!

comments.author: @LMDiscoveryGlobe
comments.text: The concept of 'Island Time'—where people don't panic over delayed ships but just patiently adapt... the modern, fast-paced world desperately needs this mindset right now.

comments.text: Isolation can sometimes protect history better than walls ever could. St. Helena proves that being far away doesn't mean being forgotten. Meanwhile, I lose my TV remote almost every week inside the same house. What part of this island's story fascinated you most?
